subcategory: Norfolk Island Pacific coastal ornamental tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in zone 9–11; Norfolk Island Hibiscus (Whitewood, Cow Itch Tree) is a handsome, symmetrical, pyramidal-form tree endemic to Norfolk Island and Lord Howe Island in the South Pacific — widely planted as a coastal street tree in Australia and California for its exceptional tolerance of salt winds, poor sandy soils, and coastal exposure — producing large, attractive, pale lavender-pink flowers throughout spring and summer that resemble hibiscus blooms.

directions: Plant Lagunaria patersonia in full sun in well-drained, lean, sandy or loamy soil; exceptionally tolerant of salt spray, coastal winds, poor soils, and drought once established — making it one of the best trees for beachfront and coastal gardens; CAUTION: the brown, dry seed pods contain fine, irritating hairs (similar to fiberglass) that cause significant skin, eye, and respiratory irritation — wear gloves and a mask when collecting or handling seed pods. Norfolk Island Hibiscus is a premier coastal street and garden tree for subtropical coastal areas — its dense, pyramidal crown, salt-spray tolerance, and resistance to sandy, nutrient-poor soils make it an ideal tree for challenging coastal garden sites where most other trees fail. The large, 3-inch, pale mauve-lavender flowers (resembling a subdued hibiscus) appear throughout spring and summer and are genuinely attractive; the tree forms a natural, neat, pyramidal shape requiring little pruning. Endemic to Norfolk Island and Lord Howe Island — both tiny, remote South Pacific islands — it has been widely planted as a coastal amenity tree across Australia's eastern coast, California, Hawaii, and Florida. In the Gulf Coast zone 9–10 garden, it performs excellently in exposed coastal positions, along salt air-exposed roadsides, and in hot, sandy inland gardens. The sole species in its genus (Lagunaria), it represents an important isolated branch of the hibiscus family.
sources: NSW Government — Lagunaria patersonia coastal planting guide
Australian Native Plants Society — Lagunaria patersonia

subcategory: Australian native drought-resistant ornamental shade tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained to dry, lean to moderately fertile, alkaline to neutral soil in zone 8–11; Kurrajong is a beautiful, long-lived, drought-hardy Australian native tree from the dry slopes, woodlands, and grasslands of New South Wales and Victoria — bearing attractive, cream-white, bell-shaped flowers with red and purple interior spotting in summer, variable, lobed or unlobed lustrous leaves, and boat-shaped woody seed pods with edible seeds — an outstanding shade tree for hot, dry garden conditions.

directions: Plant Brachychiton populneus in full sun in dry to moderately moist, well-drained, deep soil (it develops a strong taproot); it is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established and adapts to alkaline, heavy clay soils that challenge most trees; avoid overwatering, especially in youth. Kurrajong is a superlative drought-tolerant ornamental tree for hot, dry climates — native to the slopes, foothills, and grasslands of inland southeastern Australia where it is drought and heat adapted. The leaves are remarkably variable on the same tree: some deeply multi-lobed, others simple and oval, giving the tree a naturally textured, organic appearance. The cream to white, cup-shaped flowers with red and purple interior spotting are produced in summer in clusters and are attractive up close. The woody seed pods (follicles) split to reveal yellow, edible seeds surrounded by irritating hairs — the seeds are roasted to remove the hairs and then eaten or ground into flour; Aboriginal Australians roasted the seeds as a significant food and ground the inner bark fiber for fiber and string. The fat-storing capacity of the young roots was also exploited as a water source in dry conditions. A valuable shade tree for Texas, the Gulf Coast, and dry-summer climates where it withstands drought, alkaline soils, and heat that defeat most ornamental trees. Moderately frost-hardy to about 18–20°F.
sources: Australian Bush Tucker Manual — Brachychiton populneus edible uses
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Australian trees for Texas landscapes

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 9–11; Apple Geranium is a low-growing, trailing South African pelargonium — one of the most pleasantly scented of all scented geraniums — with distinctively round, soft, velvety, bright-green leaves that release an intensely fresh, sweet apple scent when touched, and small clusters of tiny white flowers with red markings throughout the warm season.

directions: Root Apple Geranium cuttings in well-drained, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; water moderately and allow to dry between waterings — it is susceptible to root rot in wet conditions; pinch stems to encourage compact growth; bring indoors as a houseplant in winter in zone 8 and colder. Apple Geranium is the most apple-scented of all the scented pelargoniums — the round, soft, velvety leaves have a pure, sweet, green apple scent with a slight minty freshness that is immediately recognizable and universally appealing. The plant has a low, spreading, trailing habit quite different from the upright rose geranium, making it excellent for container edges, hanging baskets, ground cover in sunny borders, and spilling over walls. In the kitchen, the apple-scented leaves are used to flavor jellies and jams, infused in cream or sugar for desserts, floated in cocktails and lemonade, and used to scent and flavor herbal teas. The small white flowers with pink-purple veining are charming though the plant is grown primarily for the scented foliage. Native to the southwestern Cape of South Africa in sandy, rocky, fynbos-adjacent soils, it is adapted to lean, well-drained, slightly dry conditions — performing best without heavy fertilization or overwatering. One of the most useful and fragrant herbs for the container herb garden.
sources: Herb Society of America — scented geraniums complete guide
South African Botanical Gardens — Pelargonium odoratissimum

subcategory: East African cat and dog repellent ornamental

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, moist to moderately dry soil in zone 10–12, or grow as an annual in zone 8–9; Scaredy Cat Plant (Pee-off Plant, Dog's Coleus) is a robust, strongly odorous herbaceous perennial from East Africa and South Asia — with thick, succulent-textured, toothed, bright-green leaves that emit a powerful, pungent odor of skunk and dog urine when touched — reportedly repelling cats, dogs, and other mammals from garden beds when planted around borders and problem areas.

directions: Root Plectranthus caninus cuttings or plant container stock in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; keep moderately moist during active growth; pinch tips to encourage bushy growth; the pungent odor is most effective when leaves are disturbed — plant around garden borders, vegetable beds, or areas where cat or dog activity is a problem. Scaredy Cat Plant (Coleus caninus, now correctly Plectranthus caninus) has an unusual and practical role in the garden — its powerful, highly unpleasant odor (described as a combination of skunk and dog urine, released when leaves are touched or crushed) is claimed to repel cats and dogs from treated garden areas. Scientific evidence for its effectiveness is anecdotal but widespread among gardeners who use it; it is most commonly employed as a border plant around vegetable gardens, bird feeders, and beds where cat digging and fouling are problems. The smell is quite intense when disturbed but less noticeable from a distance — making it an interesting practical solution that doesn't visually disrupt the garden. The plant itself is attractive enough: thick, slightly succulent, bright-green leaves on robust stems with small blue-purple flower spikes reminiscent of other Plectranthus. Native to tropical East Africa (Kenya, Tanzania) and India, it grows as a perennial in zone 10–12 and is easily grown as an annual from cuttings in cooler zones.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Plectranthus caninus cat repellent

subcategory: South African traditional medicinal aromatic shrub

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or sow seed in spring in full sun in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in zone 8–11; African Wormwood (Lengana, Umhlonyane in Zulu, Wilde Als in Afrikaans) is the most important traditional medicinal herb in southern Africa — a strongly aromatic, silver-green shrubby perennial native to the Afromontane grasslands, rocky slopes, and forest margins of sub-Saharan Africa — used for treating colds, flu, malaria, coughs, and stomach disorders by Zulu, Xhosa, Sotho, and Cape Malay traditional practitioners for centuries.

directions: Plant Artemisia afra in well-drained, lean, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; avoid rich, wet soils which produce lush but less aromatic and medicinally active foliage; prune back hard each spring to maintain compact, bushy form; propagate easily from cuttings in late spring. African Wormwood is the cornerstone of South African traditional herbal medicine — a deeply aromatic, silver-grey-green shrub with finely divided leaves and a characteristic powerful, camphor-dominated fragrance that is instantly recognized across southern Africa. Traditional use includes steaming the crushed leaves in a bowl of hot water for inhalation to relieve cold and flu symptoms; drinking infusions (umhlonyane tea) for fever, malaria, coughs, and stomach complaints; and rubbing the leaves on the body as an insect repellent. The plant is used by practitioners of all major South African healing traditions — Zulu izinyanga, Xhosa izangoma, Cape Malay bonesmiths — and is sold in enormous quantities at traditional medicine markets (muthi shops) throughout the country. Research has confirmed antibacterial, antifungal, and antiparasitic activity of various leaf extracts. The plant is hardy, attractive, and easy to grow in any warm, dry garden position; it makes an excellent border shrub for drought-tolerant Mediterranean-style gardens in zone 8–11. Related to the Northern Hemisphere wormwoods (Artemisia absinthium, A. annua) but distinct and more cold-hardy than expected for an African species.
sources: South African National Biodiversity Institute — Artemisia afra traditional uses

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in full sun to partial shade in fertile, moist, well-drained soil with a trellis or support in zone 9–12; Wild Cucumber Vine is a slender, fast-climbing African cucurbit vine — native to tropical Africa and naturalized widely in warm regions — producing small, white flowers and clusters of very small, oval, green fruits that ripen to brilliant scarlet-red, creating an ornamental effect similar to tiny hanging lanterns, and providing excellent wildlife value as the fruits are relished by birds.

directions: Direct sow Zehneria scabra seeds in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; provide a trellis, fence, or shrub for the slender tendrils to climb; water moderately and fertilize lightly during active growth; harvest decorative red fruits for displays or leave for birds. Wild Cucumber Vine is a vigorous, scrambling, African cucurbit vine in the Zehneria genus — a group of small, wild cucumbers native to Africa, Asia, and Australia that are less known than their cultivated relatives but often equally ornamental. The plant climbs by tendrils to 5–8 feet, producing small but numerous cream-white flowers followed by masses of tiny, glossy, oval fruits about the size of a grape that ripen from green through yellow to brilliant glossy scarlet-red, creating a cheerful, berry-like display. The fruits are not edible for humans (bitter) but are eaten enthusiastically by birds, making this an excellent plant for wildlife gardens. The rough-textured leaves (scabra means 'rough') are typical of the cucurbit family. In zone 9–12, it grows as a perennial vine from a persistent rootstock; in zone 8, it can be grown as a warm-season annual. Useful for quickly covering fences, trellises, and outbuildings in warm climates with minimal care, while providing ornamental value and wildlife support.
sources: Plants of the World Online — Zehneria scabra
South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI) — Zehneria scabra
